title: Green Stamps: The Old-School Way to Get Free Stuff
summary: Have you ever collected points from a store to get free stuff? That idea is super old! Way before smartphones and apps, there were Green Stamps. In 1896, a company called S&H started giving out small green stamps at stores. For every 10 cents you spent, you got one stamp. You would lick the stamp and stick it in a booklet. When you filled a booklet, you could trade it for prizes. One booklet could get you a lamp. Six booklets could get you a bicycle! By the 1960s, Green Stamps were everywhere. There were 800 redemption centers where you could pick your prize. Even the famous artist Andy Warhol made a painting of them. But in the 1970s, things changed. The economy got bad, and stores started their own rewards programs. By the 1980s, hardly any stores used Green Stamps. S&H tried to go digital, but it didn't work. Today, loyalty programs are bigger than ever — worth $12 billion in 2024. But here's a funny thing: people forget to use their points. In the US, about 26% of points go unused. That's $10 billion worth of free stuff left on the table!
why_it_matters: This story shows how people have always liked getting rewards, and it reminds us to use our points before they go to waste.

questions.correct_answer: The economy got bad and stores started their own programs

questions.question: In 2024, what percent of loyalty points in the US are unused?

background_read: Reward programs are a way for stores to thank you for shopping there many times.
Green Stamps were one of the very first loyalty programs, started way back in 1896.
Artist Andy Warhol, who painted famous everyday objects like soup cans, also made a painting of Green Stamps.
Article_Structure: WHO: S&H, the company that made Green Stamps, and the customers who collected them.
WHAT: Green Stamps were small stamps given with purchases that could be saved and traded for prizes.
WHERE: In stores all across the United States, with 800 redemption centers.
WHY: To make customers keep coming back to the same stores.

perspectives.description: Green Stamps made shopping more exciting because you could get free stuff like lamps or bicycles.
Many people forgot to use their stamps or lost their booklets, so they never got the prizes they earned.
Although Green Stamps are gone, the idea of loyalty programs is now bigger than ever, but on phones and apps.
